steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content:
LEFT: An independent clause ending with "and [blank]"
RIGHT: An independent clause that becomes a dependent clause when "though" precedes it

steps.title: Apply the subordinating conjunction rule
steps.content:
Rule: when a subordinating conjunction ("though," "although," "because," etc.) begins a dependent clause that follows an independent clause mid-sentence, no punctuation precedes it.

A: "...composition, though, temperature fluctuations..." — the comma after "though" incorrectly separates the conjunction from the clause it introduces, making the sentence ungrammatical. Incorrect.

B: "...composition, though: temperature fluctuations..." — a colon cannot follow a subordinating conjunction; the structure is invalid. Incorrect.

C: "...composition; though temperature fluctuations..." — a semicolon precedes the subordinating conjunction, which requires two independent clauses on each side. The "though" clause is dependent. Incorrect.

D: "...composition though temperature fluctuations..." — no punctuation before "though"; the dependent clause attaches cleanly to the independent clause. Correct.
